Existing approaches require either ground-truth verifiable rewards, restricting training to domains with automatic correctness checks (e.g., mathematics, code execution), or human preference labels, which are expensive to collect and prone to reward hacking. Recent label-free methods replace ground-truth verifiers with self-referential signals like majority voting or token entropy over a model's own outputs, but risk reinforcing a model's own errors. In this work we propose Cross-Model Entropy (CME), the mean log-likelihood of a generator's response under a separate verifier model, as a label-free reward signal for RL post-training. CME is continuous, training-free, and grounded in the principle that responses a verifier finds unsurprising are likely correct or high quality. Because the verifier is independent of the generator, the signal cannot be gamed through self-consistency. We integrate CME into GRPO with no other changes to the training loop, extending label-free RL to open-ended instruction following -- a regime where self-referential signals are inapplicable or poorly suited. On open-ended instruction following (UltraFeedback prompts, evaluated on AlpacaEval 2.0), CME rewards beat the untrained base in head-to-head LLM-as-Judge comparisons across four model families (Qwen, Llama, Gemma, OLMo) and three training regimes (pretrained, SFT, and instruction-tuned), with tie-adjusted win rates ranging from 52.5% to 71.4%. Code will be released upon publication.

</details>


### [455] [Designing Active Tether-Net Systems for Space Debris Capture with Graph-Learning-Aided Mixed-Combinatorial Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29021)
*Feng Liu,Achira Boonrath,Gishnu Madhu,Eleonora M. Botta,Souma Chowdhury*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper presents a graph-learning-aided optimization approach to tackle the Mixed Combinatorial Nonlinear Programming (MCNLP) problem in tether-net systems for capturing non-cooperative targets, demonstrating a significant improvement in convergence speed using a Graph Neural Network (GNN).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To capture large non-cooperative targets like space debris effectively, examining design and control choices of tether-net systems is essential, yet current methodologies are limited by complex nonlinear optimization challenges.

Method: The approach utilizes a Graph Neural Network (GNN) to recommend design combinations by scoring candidate nodes based on a continuous variable vector input, transforming the MCNLP into a standard Nonlinear Programming (NLP) problem.

Result: The GNN-based method exhibits faster convergence to optimal solutions compared to traditional direct methods for solving MCNLPs.

Conclusion: By employing the GNN in the optimization process, the study enhances the efficiency and effectiveness of designing tether-net systems, particularly in designing net morphology and controlling maneuverable units.

Abstract: Active tether-net systems are a promising solution for capturing large non-cooperative targets, such as space debris, by deploying a flexible net manipulated by maneuverable units (MUs). However, concurrent systematic explorations of design and control choices of the tether-net system to understand its full potential remain limited, partly due to the complex, constrained, nonlinear optimization problem that it presents -- one that involves a mixture of continuous, integer and categorical variables, with the latter two arising from net connectivity and component choices, respectively. Classical binary encoding methods are often ineffective for solving highly nonlinear and multimodal Mixed Combinatorial Nonlinear Programmings (MCNLPs) in engineering design, while integer coding approaches can introduce spurious relations among combinations. Given the graph-structured characteristics of the combinatorial space, this paper adopts and extends a new graph-learning-aided optimization approach to solve this MCNLP problem. Here, a Graph Neural Network (GNN) is trained to score (as output) and thereof recommend candidate combinations represented as nodes in a graph, with the continuous variable vector portion of a candidate design given as input. As a result, the MCNLP optimization reduces to an NLP, which can be solved using standard solvers. While this reduction approach is agnostic to the choice of the NLP solver, here a state-of-the-art Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) algorithm with gradient-based fine-tuning is used as the solver. Demonstrated on the problem of concurrently designing the morphology of the net, choice of mass and thrusters in the MUs and aiming points used by the controller of the tether-net system, the GNN-based recommender is shown to provide significantly faster convergence to similar optimal solutions, compared to direct solution of the MCNLP problem.

</details>


### [456] [Theoretical Foundations and Effective Algorithms for Policy-Aware Simulator Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29032)
*Christoph Dann,Yishay Mansour,Mehryar Mohri*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a new approach to model-based reinforcement learning that focuses on strategic robustness instead of predictive accuracy, addressing the reality gap between simulation and real-world applications.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to solve the issue of reinforcement learning optimizers exploiting model inaccuracies, which leads to a gap between simulated and real-world performance.

Method: The authors formulate a minimax game between a model player and an adversarial policy player, providing theoretical guarantees on learnability and a novel active data selection algorithm.

Result: Experiments show that this new method reduces prediction error significantly in crucial areas and allows policies to achieve close to optimal real-world performance despite being trained solely in simulation.

Conclusion: The findings indicate that focusing on strategic robustness in model learning can bridge the gap between simulation and real-world performance in reinforcement learning.

Abstract: Model-based reinforcement learning (MBRL) agents typically learn world models by minimizing predictive loss. However, powerful RL optimizers inevitably exploit minor model inaccuracies, leading to simulator exploitation and a reality gap where policies succeed in simulation but fail in the real world. We propose that the objective for learning simulators should be strategic robustness rather than predictive accuracy, and formulate this as a zero-sum minimax game between a model player and an adversarial policy player. We provide a comprehensive theoretical analysis: (1) an online learning guarantee showing the game is learnable with sublinear regret bounds; (2) a tractable critic-based simplification bounding the global policy-value gap by the local critic's loss; and (3) an Error-MDP duality, proving that finding the worst-case policy is formally dual to a standard RL problem where the reward is the one-step critic error. This duality yields a provably convergent active data selection algorithm. Experiments on continuous control tasks demonstrate that our approach reduces prediction error in strategically important regions by $1.5$-$2.2\times$ and enables policies trained purely in simulation to match near-optimal real-world performance.

</details>


### [457] [Moment Matching Q-Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29033)
*Yiyan,Liang,Sifei Liu,Weitong Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces Moment Matching Q-Learning (MoMa QL), a framework that improves the efficiency of generative models in reinforcement learning by reducing inference latency through maximum mean discrepancy for moment matching.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the prolonged inference latency in score-based and flow-based generative models that hampers their use in reinforcement learning tasks.

Method: The proposed MoMa QL uses maximum mean discrepancy (MMD) to match all orders of statistics between original and target distributions while applying strong regularization on moment statistics.

Result: MoMa QL achieves computational efficiency and demonstrates comparable performance across various D4RL tasks, particularly excelling in offline-to-online reinforcement learning due to improved action sampling speed.

Conclusion: The framework allows faster adaptation for online interactive finetuning, thereby enhancing the practical application of generative models in RL.

Abstract: Score-based and flow-based generative models exhibit remarkable expressive capacity in capturing complex distributions, and have been extensively deployed in tasks ranging from image generation to reinforcement learning. Nevertheless, these models suffer from prolonged inference latency, which imposes a significant computational bottleneck in RL with iterative sampling. To overcome this limitation, we propose a new framework named Moment Matching Q-Learning (MoMa QL), which utilizes a technique from statistical hypothesis testing known as maximum mean discrepancy (MMD) that intend to match all orders of statistics between the original and target distribution. By enforcing strong regularization on all moment statistics, this algorithm guarantees distribution-level convergence for conditional score function and remains stable under various hyperparameters. Empirically, we show that our method MoMa QL is more computationally efficient with a comparable if not competitive performance in various D4RL tasks. Remarkably, by accelerating the action sampling process for flow-based policies, MoMa QL demonstrates superior performance in offline-to-online RL tasks because of faster and stronger adaptability for online interactive finetuning.

</details>


### [458] [Parallel Adaptive Multi-Objective Evolutionary Learning of Discretized Bayesian Network Classifiers for Clinical Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29058)
*Damy M. F. Ha,Tanja Alderliesten,Peter A. N. Bosman*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Baymex, an evolutionary algorithm for learning Bayesian Networks, improves scalability and performs well on real-world clinical data, achieving efficient computation and strong predictive performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enhance Bayesian Networks' explainability in decision support, balancing multiple objectives like likelihood and accuracy.

Method: Introduced a parallelization strategy and adaptive steering for optimization while reconfiguring Baymex for multi-objective optimization of cross-entropy loss and BIC complexity for BN classifiers.

Result: Achieved speed improvements exceeding 54 times on a 16-core CPU and demonstrated competitive predictive performance against established baselines on various datasets.

Conclusion: Baymex effectively identifies compact and clinically relevant Bayesian Network classifiers suitable for real-world applications.

Abstract: Bayesian Networks (BNs) are of interest from an explainable AI viewpoint, offering transparent probabilistic models for decision support. Baymex is a recently introduced multi-objective evolutionary algorithm for learning discretized BNs, enabling experts to trade-off different objectives of interest, such as likelihood, model complexity, and prior beliefs. While Baymex has been shown to outperform state-of-the-art BN learning approaches, Baymex still 1) requires a lot of computation time and 2) has only been evaluated on synthetic data. To improve scalability, we introduce a parallelization strategy as well as a mechanism that enables adaptively steering optimization toward networks that overfit less. We furthermore reconfigure Baymex to train a BN classifier through multi-objective optimization of cross-entropy loss and the BIC complexity term so as to evaluate its performance on real-world clinical classification tasks. Besides observing speedups up to over 54 times on a 16-core CPU, comparisons against clinically familiar baselines (decision trees, logistic regression, naive Bayes, and random forests) on two open-source (RADCURE and SUPPORT) and one in-house dataset, show that Baymex obtains statistically similar or better predictive performance while producing compact, clinically inspectable BNs. Importantly, Baymex finds multiple plausible BN classifiers that contain predictors consistent with established clinical factors.

</details>


### [459] [Momentum Based Reward Design for Low Emission Traffic Signal Control](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29693)
*Chinmay Mundane,Amith Manoharan,Arun Singh*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a Momentum-Based Reward Function (MBRF) for adaptive traffic signal control using Deep Reinforcement Learning to address urban traffic congestion effectively.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve urban traffic management and reduce congestion-related issues without changing road infrastructure.

Method: The proposed MBRF focuses on encouraging continuous vehicle movement in traffic signal control, evaluated through SUMO simulations with various standard traffic metrics.

Result: MBRF yields improved throughput-emission trade-offs and demonstrates more stable learning compared to traditional delay and queue-based rewards and classical controllers.

Conclusion: Using MBRF leads to better overall traffic performance and stability in adaptive traffic signal control systems.

Abstract: Urban traffic congestion is a growing global issue contributing significantly to long commute times and environmental pollution. Traditional traffic signal control systems often fail to adapt to dynamic traffic conditions. Adaptive traffic signal control can improve urban traffic without changing road infrastructure. Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) has shown strong performance for this task, but existing delay and queue-based rewards often produce short-sighted or unstable policies. This paper proposes a Momentum-Based Reward Function (MBRF) that encourages vehicles to keep moving rather than penalizing congestion alone. The method is evaluated in SUMO (Simulation of Urban MObility) using standard traffic metrics such as waiting time, queue length, throughput, and CO2 emissions. Results show that the proposed reward produces better throughput-emission trade-offs and more stable learning behavior than delay or queue-based rewards, as well as classical controllers such as Max Pressure and LQF.

</details>


### [460] [Ensemble Score Filtering for Real-Data Energy Consumption Forecast Correction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29072)
*Ruoyu Hu,Dahai Yu,Feng Bao,Guang Wang,Guannan Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This study addresses the challenges of accurate energy consumption forecasting under incomplete data by employing the Ensemble Score Filter (EnSF) for improved state estimation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Accurate energy consumption forecasting is critical for power system operations, but real-world data can be incomplete and noisy, which necessitates advanced forecasting models and correction methods.

Method: The Ensemble Score Filter is used to assimilate partial observations and correct forecasts, leveraging a pretrained spatio-temporal model as the state propagator, and employing score-based diffusion models for approximation.

Result: Numerical experiments reveal that the EnSF significantly enhances state estimation compared to open-loop propagation and outperforms the Ensemble Kalman Filter in the nonlinear observation context.

Conclusion: The use of EnSF allows for reliable corrections in forecasting models, thus providing more accurate energy consumption predictions despite challenges with data quality.

Abstract: Accurate estimation and forecasting of energy consumption are important for power-system operation, planning, and demand-side management. In practice, however, complete and timely measurements may not always be available, and the observed data can be partial, noisy, or delayed. This motivates the use of learned forecasting models for predicting the evolving consumption state, together with data assimilation methods for sequential forecast correction. In this work, we study a high-dimensional data assimilation problem for real energy-consumption data. \modeltext{The forward prediction is supplied by a pretrained black-box spatio-temporal forecasting model, which is treated as the state propagator in the filtering procedure.} We employ the Ensemble Score Filter (EnSF) to assimilate partial and noisy observations and to correct the forecast trajectory over time. The EnSF uses score-based diffusion models to approximate filtering distributions and avoids retraining neural-network score models during assimilation by using a closed-form score representation and Monte Carlo approximation. Numerical experiments demonstrate that open-loop propagation of the learned forecasting model can become unreliable over long horizons, while EnSF-based correction substantially improves state estimation. Comparisons with the Ensemble Kalman Filter (EnKF) further show that EnSF provides stronger correction under the nonlinear observation setting considered in this work.

</details>


### [461] [OISD: On-Policy Internal Self-Distillation of Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29089)
*Xinyu Liu,Darryl Cherian Jacob,Yang Zhou,Jindong Wang,Pan He*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces the On-Policy Internal Self-Distillation (OISD) framework for enhancing reasoning in reinforcement learning by utilizing predictive signals from the output policy to improve intermediate representations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the limitation of existing RL post-training methods that focus primarily on final outputs and neglect the valuable intermediate representation signals for reasoning.

Method: The OISD framework uses two complementary alignment mechanisms—logit and attention alignment—to transfer knowledge from the final layer (acting as a policy and an internal teacher) to intermediate layers during both rollout and Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) optimization.

Result: Experimental results indicate that OISD significantly improves reasoning capabilities over existing strong RL baselines in mathematical tasks, demonstrating its effectiveness.

Conclusion: The study concludes that the OISD framework enhances policy learning by distilling informative intermediate representations while maintaining policy consistency, paving the way for better performance in reasoning tasks.

Abstract: Recent reinforcement learning (RL) post-training approaches primarily optimize the final output policy using sparse outcome-level rewards, while largely overlooking predictive signals encoded in intermediate representations. In this paper, we introduce a new paradigm called on-policy internal self-distillation and propose the OISD framework, which improves reasoning by transferring on-policy predictive signals from the final layer to intermediate representations. During rollout and Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) optimization, the final layer acts as both the policy and a detached internal teacher for selected intermediate layers, which are guided to align with it through two complementary mechanisms: logit alignment, which transfers high-level reasoning behaviors (how to think), and attention alignment, which enforces consistent attention patterns (where to look) from the final layer to the selected intermediate layer, both without requiring external privileged information. Our OISD, together with GRPO, employs signed advantage-weighted Jensen--Shannon alignment to distill informative intermediate representations while preserving policy consistency under a unified acting policy. Experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of OISD, with substantial and consistent improvements over strong reasoning RL baselines across four mathematical reasoning tasks. The code will be released at https://github.com/THE-MALT-LAB/OISD

</details>


### [462] [Model Merging by Output-Space Projection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29101)
*Bethan Evans,Benjamin Etheridge,Stephen Roberts,Jared Tanner*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper presents a novel approach to model merging by formulating it as a convex quadratic program over residual updates, offering a method that circumvents heuristic designs while providing empirical performance and diagnostic capabilities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve upon existing model merging techniques that lack formal guarantees and rely on heuristics, proposing a more optimal and systematic approach.

Method: The merging process is treated as a convex quadratic programme that minimizes a squared-output calibration objective, integrating existing methods as special cases and facilitating a diagnostic for merge quality prediction.

Result: The proposed quadratic programming framework achieves comparable or superior results to existing methods in single-layer models, with additional diagnostics to predict merge quality, and extends effectively to multi-layer models.

Conclusion: The method demonstrates consistent performance improvements across benchmarks in both language and vision tasks, validating the efficacy of the approach over simpler alternatives.

Abstract: Model merging combines fine-tuned checkpoints into a single multi-task model without retraining. Existing methods - such as task arithmetic, model soups, TIES, and DARE - are computationally efficient and empirically successful, but rely on heuristic design choices and lack formal optimality guarantees. We show that merging can be formulated as a convex quadratic programme over residual updates, yielding weights that minimise a squared-output calibration objective using calibration inputs and fine-tuned model outputs, and subsuming existing methods as special cases. Our framework yields a closed-form diagnostic - the fraction of residual energy captured by a chosen basis - that predicts downstream merge quality using only the calibration set. Empirically, the QP matches or outperforms existing methods in the single-layer setting, and we characterise when the optimal basis provides significant gains over the cheaper diagonal QP. We extend to multi-layer merging via a sequential layer-wise algorithm and demonstrate consistent gains across language and vision benchmarks.

</details>


### [463] [Bridging Chemists and AI: An Expert-Augmented Framework for Interpretable Route Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29108)
*Yujia Guo,Mikhail Kabeshov,Tat Hong Duong Le,Samuel Genheden,Marco V. Mijangos,Varvara Voinarvoska,Giulia Bergonzini,Ola Engkvist,Samuel Kaski*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A novel expert-augmented, data-driven framework enhances the assessment of multi-step synthetic routes in organic synthesis through machine learning integration, achieving significantly improved predictive accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to improve the efficiency and decision-making process in selecting synthetic routes in organic synthesis, addressing limitations of existing data-driven systems that overlook multi-objectivity and rely on inadequate datasets.

Method: We introduce a scoring framework that combines machine learning, specifically a DeepSets-based model, with expert chemist evaluations to assess synthetic routes. The model is trained on tree edit distances and fine-tuned to categorize routes into Good, Plausible, and Bad categories.

Result: The system achieved high correlation coefficients (Spearman: 0.78, Pearson: 0.77) for predicting categorical assessments and a 60.2% accuracy for numerical score predictions, substantially better than the previous baseline of 17.5%.

Conclusion: The proposed framework effectively integrates expert knowledge with machine learning, resulting in enhanced assessment capabilities for multi-step synthetic route selection.

Abstract: Selecting efficient multi-step synthetic routes is a central challenge in organic synthesis, particularly in medicinal and process chemistry, where route choice directly impacts feasibility, cost, and development efficiency. Data-driven assessment systems often oversimplify the multi-objective nature of synthesis design and rely on proxy datasets, such as patent routes, rather than universally grounded criteria. To address this, we introduce an expert-augmented, data-driven scoring framework that integrates machine learning with chemists' domain knowledge for both numerical and explainable route assessment. A DeepSets-based model is trained using tree edit distance between reference and machine-generated routes, and then fine-tuned with expert evaluations to produce both quantitative scores and interpretable qualitative categories: Good, Plausible, and Bad. The resulting system achieves a Spearman correlation coefficient of 0.78 and a Pearson correlation of 0.77 for category assessment prediction, and 60.2% top-1 ranking accuracy for score prediction, substantially outperforming the previous baseline of 17.5%.

</details>


### [464] [When and How Long? The Readout-Mediator Angle in Temporal Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29126)
*Shreyas Fadnavis,Praitayini Kanakaraj,Felix Wyss*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Linear probes may decode representations well but can be orthogonal to model usage. A specific probe fails to affect model performance, while other methods do. This highlights a general issue in probe-based interpretability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate the relationship between linear probes and model computation in understanding representations used by language models, specifically in calendar-date duration reasoning.

Method: The study utilizes a $	ext{sin}/	ext{cos}$ probe to analyze layer activations and compares the effects of ablation of the probe with ablation of a four-dimensional subspace identified via Distributed Alignment Search (DAS). It measures the readout-mediator angle between these subspaces and employs sparse-autoencoder decomposition to examine feature encoding.

Result: The research finds that the probe learns an orthogonal direction to the actual model's computation, evidenced by the readout-mediator angle resembling random subspaces and a lack of overlap in features encoding distinct concepts across various models and domains.

Conclusion: Probe-based interpretability is flawed, as high-confidence reports from probes can indicate that the model has effectively disregarded the directions learned by the probe, posing risks for their use in safety monitoring.

Abstract: A linear probe can decode a representation almost perfectly and yet be completely irrelevant to how the model uses it. On calendar-date duration reasoning in language models, a $\sin$/$\cos$ probe recovers day-of-year from a layer's activations, yet ablating its direction has no effect on the model's answers -- while ablating a four-dimensional subspace found by Distributed Alignment Search (DAS) at the same layer collapses performance entirely. We measure the angle between these two subspaces -- the \emph{readout-mediator angle} -- and find it indistinguishable from the angle between two random subspaces (the Haar-uniform null), meaning the probe has learned a direction orthogonal to the model's actual computation. Reverse-engineering the circuit reveals why: attention heads route month-grained context through learned QK offsets at ${\pm}30$ and ${\pm}61$ days, and MLPs then convert \emph{when} (absolute date) into \emph{how long} (duration) -- all downstream of the causal subspace the probe never touches. Sparse-autoencoder decomposition confirms the split: probe-aligned and DAS-aligned features encode semantically disjoint concepts with negligible causal overlap. The dissociation replicates across four scales ($1.5$-$9\,$B) and two model families, with preliminary evidence on two further domains (spatial displacement, symbolic arithmetic), suggesting that readout-mediator orthogonality is a general failure mode of probe-based interpretability. This directly undermines proposals to deploy probes as runtime safety monitors: the probe can report high confidence on a direction the model has silently abandoned.

</details>


### [465] [Apertus LLM Family Expansion via Distillation and Quantization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29128)
*Andrei Panferov,Davit Melikidze,Martin Jaggi,Dan Alistarh*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper presents the development of a distilled family of models (Apertus-v1.1) as a cost-effective solution for expanding LLMs to meet diverse hardware constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the diverse hardware and budget constraints associated with the widespread adoption of large language models (LLMs).

Method: The authors use distillation and quantization techniques on the Apertus 8B LLM to create a family of distilled models (Apertus-v1.1) with up to 4B parameters, trained on permissive license tokens.

Result: The resulting models demonstrate strong accuracy performance while being cost-efficient, effectively covering a wide range of hardware requirements.

Conclusion: The approach validates distillation and quantization as effective methods for creating a variety of LLMs tailored to different hardware formats.

Abstract: The wide adoption of LLMs has led to their use in great variety of applications and scenarios, such as chatbot assistants and data annotation, creating the need for the models to satisfy certain budget and hardware constraints. This has led to the trend of LLMs being released in batches consisting of similar models of various sizes for the family of models to adhere to as wide of a range of constraints as possible. In this paper, we validate distillation and quantization as a cost-effective way to expand model families to new sizes and hardware formats. Based on the open-recipe Apertus 8B LLM, we produce Apertus-v1.1 - a distilled family of models with up to 4B parameters trained on 1.7T permissive license tokens. We demonstrate cost-efficiency and strong accuracy performance of our approach for covering large ranges of hardware and systems requirements.

</details>


### [466] [Optimal Gap-Dependent Regret for Private Stochastic Decision-Theoretic Online Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29148)
*Tommaso Cesari,Roberto Colomboni*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper presents a new algorithm for stochastic decision-theoretic online learning that achieves a regret bound under event-level pure differential privacy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the COLT open problem regarding optimal regret rates for stochastic decision-theoretic online learning with privacy constraints.

Method: The proposed algorithm is horizon-free, partitions time into exponentially sized blocks, and selects actions via an exponential mechanism on a random prefix of the previous block.

Result: Establishes a regret bound of 1000 times the known lower bound, specifically relating to the minimal gap and privacy parameter, for any time horizon T.

Conclusion: The algorithm effectively balances regret minimization and privacy preservation, achieving substantial theoretical guarantees.

Abstract: We study stochastic decision-theoretic online learning with full information and event-level pure differential privacy. A COLT open problem of Hu and Mehta asks to determine the optimal gap-dependent regret rate for stochastic decision-theoretic online learning under pure event-level differential privacy. For $K$ actions, losses in $[0,1]$, and a unique best action separated from the second-best action by gap $Δ_{\min}$, the known lower bound is of order $
  \frac{\log K}{\min\{Δ_{\min},\varepsilon\}}, $
or equivalently, up to universal constants, of order \[
  \frac{\log K}{Δ_{\min}}+\frac{\log K}{\varepsilon}. \] We give a horizon-free pure-DP algorithm and prove the explicit regret bound \[
  \operatorname{Reg}_T
  \le
  1000 \cdot \left(\frac{\log K}{Δ_{\min}}+\frac{\log K}{\varepsilon}\right) \] for every horizon $T$. The numerical constant is not optimized. The algorithm partitions time into blocks of exponentially increasing size, plays a single action throughout each block, and chooses the next action by an exponential mechanism applied to a data-independent random prefix of the previous block. The random prefix converts block regret into a sum, over all prefix lengths, of softmax selection errors. A single entropy-potential argument controls all privacy-dominated large-gap actions at cost $\log K/\varepsilon$.

</details>


### [467] [Do Deep Networks Forget Initialization? A Forgetting-Time View of Practical Inductive Bias](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29152)
*Mohua Das,Pierfrancesco Beneventano,Shibshankar Dey,Gareth H. McKinkey,Tomaso Poggio*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper investigates the persistence of initial bias in neural networks after training, introducing the concept of initialization memory and illustrating its impact through controlled experiments with SGD and Adam optimizers on ResNet architectures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how much bias from randomly initialized neural networks remains after training and how different training methods affect this bias.

Method: Controlled CIFAR-10 experiments on ResNet-9 and ResNet architectures with varying initialization scales while applying different training regimes using SGD and Adam optimizers.

Result: Significant variance in test accuracy (up to 26.5 percentage points) exists across different initialization scales with low-learning-rate SGD, while Adam methods diminish this effect. This variance is consistent regardless of extended training epochs.

Conclusion: The practical inductive bias of trained networks relies not only on the initial architectural prior but also on how training dynamics filter this bias, with regularizers that enhance generalization being the same that reduce initialization memory.

Abstract: Randomly initialized neural networks induce a prior over functions, but the predictor used in practice is produced only after training. We ask how much of this initial bias survives the training pipeline. To make the question measurable, we introduce initialization memory: the dependence of the validation-selected predictor on the scale of the random initialization. We perform controlled CIFAR-10 experiments on ResNets where initialization memory already sharply separates training regimes. Low-learning-rate SGD can interpolate while still remembering its initialization: on ResNet-9 with batch size $b=128$, test accuracy varies by $26.5$ percentage points across initialization scales despite $\ge99.5\%$ training accuracy. This is not undertraining: extending the same low-learning-rate regime to $5{,}000$ epochs leaves the spread essentially unchanged. In contrast, Adam-family methods largely erase the dependence. SGD can also be made to forget when larger learning rates are paired with explicit $L_2$ norm control. We interpret these findings in terms of the time scale of forgetting: gradient-flow-like dynamics can preserve initialization memory, whereas stochastic finite-step effects, explicit norm decay, and adaptive preconditioning erase it on scales governed by the size of explicit or implicit regularization. The practical inductive bias of a trained network is therefore not the architectural prior alone, but the architectural prior after being filtered by the forgetting dynamics of the training pipeline; and the same regularizers that improve generalization are precisely those that erase memory of initialization.

</details>


### [468] [Unveiling Multi-regime Patterns in SciML: Distinct Failure Modes and Regime-specific Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29153)
*Yuxin Wang,Yuanzhe Hu,Xiaokun Zhong,Xiaopeng Wang,Haiquan Lu,Tianyu Pang,Michael W. Mahoney,Yujun Yan,Pu Ren,Yaoqing Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper explores multi-regime behavior in scientific machine learning models, identifying consistent regime structures, regime-specific optimization effectiveness, and unique failure modes, all validated across various benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how different hyperparameter settings lead to varying training regimes in SciML models and how these regimes influence performance and robustness.

Method: A regime-aware diagnostic framework is developed to analyze performance, training dynamics, and loss-landscape geometry across different SciML models and configurations.

Result: The study identifies a consistent three-regime structure, demonstrates that optimization methods are not universally effective across all regimes, and reveals nuanced failure modes in SciML models.

Conclusion: The findings support a unified perspective on challenges in SciML and suggest that regime-aware strategies are essential for enhancing model robustness.

Abstract: Neural networks trained under different hyperparameter settings can fall into distinct training "regimes," with consistent behavior within regimes and qualitative differences across regimes. In this paper, we study such multi-regime behavior in scientific machine learning (SciML) models through a regime-aware diagnostic framework that jointly analyzes performance, training dynamics, and loss-landscape geometry. We identify three key findings: (i) a consistent three-regime structure emerges across many standard SciML models, different constraint enforcements, and various optimizer designs; (ii) optimization effectiveness is regime-specific, with no single method performing well across all regimes; and (iii) SciML models can exhibit fine-grained failure modes that can challenge conventional interpretations of standard loss-landscape metrics. Our results provide an approach to establish a unified, task-oblivious perspective on failure modes in SciML and to inform regime-aware guidance for improving robustness. We validate these findings across widely-used SciML models, including physics-informed neural networks, neural operators, and neural ordinary differential equations, on benchmarks spanning representative ordinary and partial differential equations.

</details>


### [469] [Knowledge Offloading: Decomposing LLMs into Sparse Backbones and Memory Modules](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29075)
*Karim Galliamov,Rochelle Choenni,Ivan Titov*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces knowledge offloading (KOFF), a method to reorganize LLMs by separating general capabilities from domain-specific knowledge, achieving effective pruning while maintaining overall performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To explore the potential of reorganizing large language models (LLMs) by offloading specialized knowledge to external memory modules.

Method: KOFF framework decomposes pretrained LLMs into a shared backbone and domain-specific memories, utilizing structured pruning and lightweight recovery modules like LoRA adapters and learned key-value caches.

Result: KOFF enables substantial knowledge offloading from the shared backbone to external memories with minimal performance loss, notably preserving performance even at 12% global sparsity, while traditional pruning without memories leads to performance degradation.

Conclusion: The study demonstrates that LLM capabilities can be effectively redistributed between a shared core and external memories, allowing for efficient specialization in language tasks.

Abstract: LLMs encode both general capabilities and domain-specific knowledge in a single set of parameters. We ask whether this capacity can be reorganized: keeping broadly useful computation in a shared backbone, while moving specialized knowledge into external memory modules. We propose \emph{knowledge offloading} (KOFF), a framework for decomposing a pretrained LLM into a sparse shared backbone and domain-specific memories. Starting from a frozen base model, we jointly learn a structured pruning mask and lightweight recovery modules, implemented as LoRA adapters and learned key-value caches. Across Llama and Qwen models from 3B to 8B, we find that non-trivial capacity can be moved out of the shared backbone without a large loss in model ability. At around 12\% global sparsity, KOFF preserves much of the unpruned model's performance, while pruning the same frozen model without memories degrades sharply. Ablations show that LoRA and learned KV memories are complementary, and specialization analyses suggest that the learned decomposition is meaningful: language-specific neurons are preferentially removed while language-general neurons largely remain in the backbone. These results suggest that knowledge can be reallocated between a shared core and swappable external memories.

</details>


### [470] [RUBRIC-ARROW: Alternating Pointwise Rubric Reward Modeling for LLM Post-training in Non-verifiable Domains](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29156)
*Haoxiang Jiang,Zihan Dong,Tianci Liu,Wanying Wang,Ran Xu,Tony Yu,Linjun Zhang,Haoyu Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RUBRIC-ARROW is an innovative framework for pointwise reward modeling in LLM post-training that addresses the challenges of subjective scoring and ties in evaluations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enhance pointwise reward modeling in subjective settings by overcoming the challenges of absolute scoring and ties in evaluations.

Method: RUBRIC-ARROW jointly trains a rubric generator and a rubric-conditioned judge using pairwise preference data, employing a probability-based scoring rule and an alternating GRPO scheme.

Result: The framework demonstrates competitive reward-modeling accuracy compared to existing methods and improves results in downstream policy post-training.

Conclusion: RUBRIC-ARROW effectively addresses the limitations of traditional rubric-based evaluation methods, providing reliable and robust performance in reward modeling.

Abstract: Pointwise reward modeling offers critical signals for LLM post-training, yet struggles with absolute scoring in subjective, non-verifiable settings. Rubric-based methods address this by decomposing evaluation into explicit criteria, but existing approaches typically depend on frontier LLMs and suffer from ties caused by hard Boolean aggregation. We present RUBRIC-ARROW, an alternating framework that jointly trains a rubric generator and a rubric-conditioned judge, with its RL stage using only pairwise preference data. Our method couples a probability-based scoring rule that reduces ties with phase-specific preference-based rewards and an alternating GRPO scheme that together train the pointwise evaluator. Extensive experiments show that RUBRIC-ARROW achieves competitive reward-modeling accuracy and yields consistent gains for downstream policy post-training.

</details>


### [471] [Parallax: Parameterized Local Linear Attention for Language Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29157)
*Yifei Zuo,Dhruv Pai,Zhichen Zeng,Alec Dewulf,Shuming Hu,Zhaoran Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Parallax introduces a scalable parameterized Local Linear Attention (LLA) mechanism for large language models (LLMs), enhancing efficiency and performance over existing methods like FlashAttention.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The need for improved attention mechanisms in LLMs to address computational and numerical stability issues while enhancing performance.

Method: Parallax replaces the numerical solver in LLA with a learnable query-like projector for efficient KV covariance probing, connected to other attention mechanisms via bandwidth and structure.

Result: Parallax matches or outperforms FlashAttention 2/3 across various configurations and shows consistent perplexity improvements during pretraining, transferring gains to downstream tasks.

Conclusion: Parallax offers a Pareto improvement in LLM attention mechanisms, and the study highlights novel architecture-optimizer interactions not previously documented.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have become the central paradigm in artificial intelligence, yet the core computational primitive of attention has remained structurally unchanged. Local Linear Attention (LLA) is an attention mechanism derived from nonparametric statistics in the test-time regression framework. In contrast to prior research on efficient attention variants, LLA upgrades the local constant estimate in softmax attention to a local linear estimate, yielding provably superior bias-variance tradeoffs for associative memory. However, LLA has not been scaled in LLM pretraining due to computational and numerical stability concerns. We introduce Parallax, a parameterized Local Linear Attention that is scalable for LLMs. Parallax eliminates the numerical solver in LLA and learns an extra query-like projector that probes the KV covariance. We place Parallax within a family of attention mechanisms connected by the bandwidth, the probe construction and the affine structure. We propose a hardware-aware algorithm that increases the arithmetic intensity over FlashAttention, shifting attention into a more compute bound regime. Our prototype decode kernel matches or outperforms FlashAttention 2/3 across diverse batch sizes and context lengths. We pretrain Parallax at 0.6B and 1.7B scales and find consistent perplexity improvements throughout pretraining with gains that transfer to downstream benchmarks. The advantage persists under both parameter-matched and compute-matched controls, demonstrating a Pareto improvement. We perform careful pretraining ablations and identify a novel phenomenon whereby Muon unlocks the capacity of Parallax. To our knowledge, this is the first empirical demonstration of strong architecture-optimizer codesign for attention mechanisms in the architecture research literature.

</details>


### [472] [Evolutionary Refinement of Generative Graph Topologies: A Hybrid WGAN-GA Approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29161)
*James Sargant,Seyedeh Ava Razi Razavi,Renata Dividino,Sheridan Houghten*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This study enhances GAN-based graph generation by incorporating a Genetic Algorithm for edge refinement, improving the realism of generated graphs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address deviations in structural properties of GAN-generated graphs compared to real graphs, which affects their realism.

Method: The authors integrate a Genetic Algorithm to refine edges in graphs generated by a GAN, using a GNN-based critic for evaluation and ensuring class consistency.

Result: GA refinement reduces Maximum Mean Discrepancy (MMD) between generated and real graphs, indicating improved structural alignment.

Conclusion: Evolutionary refinement via Genetic Algorithms effectively corrects deviations in GAN-generated graphs, enhancing their realism and applicability in data augmentation.

Abstract: Generating realistic graph-structured data is challenging due to discrete connectivity, varying graph sizes, and class-specific structural patterns. Recent Generative Adversarial Networks (GAN)-based graph generation methods improve edge modelling by learning connectivity and matching class-specific density distributions. However these models still exhibit noticeable deviations such as in degree and spectral distribution when compared to real graphs, indicating that important structural properties are not fully preserved. This work aims to reduce these deviations by refining the graphs produced by an existing GAN-based graph generator framework with a Genetic Algorithm (GA). In the GAN framework, the generator produces both node features and connectivity patterns, while a GNN-based critic evaluates graph realism and class consistency to ensure global structural and class alignment. Building on this foundation, we apply a GA to refine the edges of generated graphs. The refinement process guides synthetic graphs toward closer agreement with real data, while preserving diversity and novelty. Experimental results show that the GA refinement consistently lowers combined Maximum Mean Discrepancy (MMD) compared to the base model, leading to graphs that more closely match real structural patterns. This demonstrates that evolutionary refinement is an effective and flexible way to correct residual structural deviations in GAN-based graph generators, improving their suitability for realistic graph synthesis and data augmentation.

</details>


### [473] [Probabilistic bias adjustment of seasonal forecasts using generative machine learning: A case study of Arctic sea ice predictions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29172)
*Parsa Gooya,Reinel Sospedra-Alfonso*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper presents an advanced probabilistic post-processing framework for seasonal climate predictions using conditional Variational Autoencoders (cVAEs) to improve Arctic sea ice forecasts by generating large, bias-corrected ensembles with better calibration and resolution.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enhance seasonal climate forecasts by addressing systematic biases and resolution issues in standard ensemble predictions, particularly for Arctic sea ice.

Method: A modified cVAE framework is utilized, replacing the Gaussian decoder with a generator, optimizing the objective function using Continuous Ranked Probability Score instead of Mean Squared Error, and leveraging higher resolution target datasets.

Result: The results indicate that the new forecasts are better calibrated, more aligned with observed distributions, and surpass benchmark predictions in terms of prediction accuracy and resolution.

Conclusion: The modified framework shows significant improvements in seasonal climate prediction accuracy and resolution, particularly for Arctic sea ice forecasts, addressing known limitations of traditional cVAEs.

Abstract: Seasonal climate predictions support planning and risk management by offering early information of the most likely-to-occur climate conditions in the coming months, and associated uncertainties. Ensemble forecasts enable this by simulating many plausible outcomes, allowing predictions to be expressed as usable probabilities. Large ensembles and high-resolution forecasts strengthen this guidance by better sampling uncertainty and capturing finer-scale processes but come with significant computational cost. Moreover, forecast ensembles drift and exhibit systematic biases and spatio-temporal errors that grow with lead time, requiring careful post-processing and calibration. A probabilistic post-processing framework based on conditional Variational Autoencoders (cVAEs) was developed at the Canadian Center for Climate Modeling and Analysis to generate large ensembles of bias adjusted seasonal predictions of Arctic sea ice. The generative model was designed to learn the observational distribution conditioned on the biased model prediction. This enables generation of arbitrarily large ensembles of well-calibrated, bias corrected forecasts with improved skill. Here, we extend this framework to address the loss of fine-scale energy and the characteristic blurriness in predictions, a known limitation of standard cVAEs. Specifically, we employ a generator in place of the Gaussian parametrized decoder in the cVAE and use Continuous Ranked Probability Score in the objective function instead of the Mean Square Error. We further use a higher resolution target dataset compared to the raw forecast. We show that the adjusted forecasts are better calibrated, more consistent with the observational distribution, and exhibit smaller errors than benchmark predictions, while also enhancing the resolution of the raw forecasts and improving sharpness and spectral power relative to the standard cVAE.

</details>


### [474] [TIMEGATE: Sustainable Time-Boxed Promotion Gates for Continual ML Adaptation Under Resource Constraints](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29183)
*Abhijit Chakrabroty,Suddhasvatta Das,Kevin A. Gary,Yash Shah*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introducing TIMEGATE, a policy layer for efficient machine learning adaptation managing compute, labeling, training, and evaluation costs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To optimize the continual adaptation process in machine learning systems by efficiently managing resources and evaluation processes.

Method: TIMEGATE manages the budgets for time, labeling, training, and evaluation, utilizing a metric-availability signal (M) for decision-making.

Result: Labeling in TIMEGATE offers a 2.3x performance improvement on Adult tabular data and enhances SST-2 results from 0.80 to 0.96 with notable efficiency.

Conclusion: Implementing TIMEGATE can lead to substantial savings in evaluation compute and energy while maintaining effectiveness across multiple tasks.

Abstract: As machine learning(ML) systems evolve to continual adaptation, each re-training cycle uses compute, annotation, and energy. We introduce TIMEGATE, a policy layer managing adaptation by budgeting time, labeling, training, and evaluation. TIMEGATE emits a metric-availability signal M for partial vs. full-evaluation decisions. We validate: (i) labeling outperforms training by 2.3x on Adult tabular; (ii) it transfers to LLaMA-3.1-8B + QLoRA on SST-2 (accuracy 0.80 to 0.96; M =1 in 35/36 runs); (iii) M is informative, 28-cell sensitivity shows M drops to 0.81 at tight thresholds; (iv) 100-cycle simulation achieves 66% evaluation-compute savings with no silent mis-promotions; (v) 10%-slice evaluation on LLaMA uses 89% less wall-clock and energy on a single H200 (ratios agree to 0.2%).

</details>


### [475] [Influence-Guided Symbolic Regression: Scientific Discovery via LLM-Driven Equation Search with Granular Feedback](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29184)
*Evgeny S. Saveliev,Samuel Holt,Nabeel Seedat,David L. Bentley,Jim Weatherall,Mihaela van der Schaar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: IGSR enhances symbolic regression through an iterative process combining LLM-generated functions and influence score evaluation for better model selection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address limitations of LLMs in symbolic regression due to inefficient search strategies and the use of scalar performance metrics that obscure component contributions.

Method: IGSR uses an iterative two-step process where an LLM generates candidate functions that are evaluated with granular influence scores, facilitating a pruning process and integrating with Monte Carlo Tree Search for optimal exploration.

Result: The method showed effectiveness across various benchmarks, including pharmacological models and real genomic data, leading to significant discoveries; notably, identifying a novel link between DNA methylation and RNA Polymerase II pausing.

Conclusion: IGSR provides a robust framework for improved symbolic regression, enabling genuine scientific discovery and validation through experimental support.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) offer a promising avenue for scientific discovery, yet their application to symbolic regression is often constrained by inefficient search strategies and coarse feedback signals. Current methods typically guide LLMs using scalar metrics (e.g., global Mean Squared Error), which fail to identify which components of a proposed equation are driving performance or causing error. We introduce \textit{Influence-Guided Symbolic Regression} (IGSR), a method that frames equation discovery as an iterative two-step process combining diverse term generation with rigorous selection: an LLM generates candidate basis functions $ψ_j(\mathbf{x})$ for a linear model, which are then evaluated using granular influence scores $Δ_j$. These scores quantify each term's marginal contribution to generalization accuracy, enabling an influence-guided pruning process that systematically refines the model structure. Integrating this mechanism into a Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) enables navigating the combinatorial search space while balancing exploration of novel functional forms with exploitation of high-influence components. We demonstrate IGSR's effectiveness on a diverse suite of benchmarks, including LLM-SRBench, pharmacological PKPD models, an epidemiological simulation, and real-world genomic data. Notably, we validate the framework's capacity for genuine discovery in a case study using a high-dimensional biological dataset, in which IGSR identified a novel relationship between DNA methylation and RNA Polymerase II pausing; a hypothesis that was subsequently supported via wet-lab experimentation.

</details>


### [476] [When RL Suppresses Its Own Vocabulary: Recovering Reasoning Diversity in Puzzle-to-Math Transfer](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29190)
*Mayug Maniparambil,Arjun Karuvally,Terrence Sejnowski,Fergal Reid*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper explores cross-domain transfer in RLVR, achieving improved reasoning in LLMs through a framework that analyzes reasoning primitives and introduces a novelty bonus to enhance performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards enhances reasoning capabilities in LLMs across different domains, especially in the absence of direct mathematics problems during training.

Method: A reasoning primitive-level framework is utilized, involving a 9-class span classifier and motif extraction techniques to dissect reasoning processes into primitive motifs, followed by a novelty bonus approach to foster diversity among correct rollouts.

Result: The study reveals that SFT on constraint-satisfaction puzzles creates a reasoning vocabulary that boosts performance on complex tasks, especially showing a total increase of 20 percentage points in pass rates on a math benchmark through various training adjustments.

Conclusion: The end-to-end methodology significantly elevates the model's capability in hard mathematics problems, achieving a pass rate improvement from 16.0% to 36.0%, without incorporating math-related training during earlier stages.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning using verifiable rewards (RLVR) improves LLM reasoning, but the conditions under which it transfers across domains -- and why it does so -- remain under-explored. We study cross-domain transfer in a 7B model whose SFT and RL post-training stages use only constraint-satisfaction puzzles, with no mathematics problems in the post-training data. To analyze how transfer emerges, we introduce a reasoning primitive-level framework that combines a 9-class span classifier with motif extraction, allowing us to segment chain-of-thought traces into primitive motifs and track their evolution across training stages and domains. We find that puzzle SFT induces a reasoning-primitive vocabulary, yielding a $+7$pp \texttt{pass@32} gain on OlymMATH-Hard. Vanilla GSPO then composes these primitives into longer compute-verify chains, adding a further $+6$pp. However, this RL stage also suppresses exploratory primitives such as \textit{hypothesize} and \textit{backtrack}. To address this, we introduce a novelty bonus that rewards diverse correct rollouts, using perplexity under the reference model as a signal. This restores recovery primitives during RL and adds a further $+7$pp \texttt{pass@32} relative to vanilla GSPO. Finally, the end-to-end recipe raises the hard-math capability ceiling from $16.0\%$ at the OLMo3-7B-Instruct-SFT base to $36.0\%$, without adding any mathematics problems during the SFT or RL stages.

</details>


### [477] [Stochastic Lifting for Generating Trajectories of Stochastic Physical Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29194)
*Jules Berman,Tobias Blickhan,Benjamin Peherstorfer*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper presents Stochastic Lifting, a method for modeling state transitions in stochastic systems by incorporating high-dimensional random labels, allowing for diverse trajectory generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenge of predicting multiple plausible next states in stochastic physical systems while preventing the model from collapsing to a mean prediction.

Method: Stochastic Lifting attaches independent high-dimensional random labels to each state transition, fitting a transition map using regression from the current state and labels to the next state.

Result: The method allows the model to represent multiple plausible next states from similar current states, generating diverse trajectories at inference by sampling fresh labels at each time step.

Conclusion: Stochastic Lifting effectively improves the representation of state transitions in stochastic systems, facilitating the generation of varied trajectories.

Abstract: Many stochastic physical systems evolve smoothly over time in the sense that the distribution of states changes regularly across time steps. The transition from current state to the next state can often be modeled as the combination of a smooth map and an explicit source of randomness. Stochastic Lifting exploits this structure by attaching an independent, high-dimensional random label to each state transition in the training data and fitting a transition map from the current state and label to the next state using a standard regression loss. The labels act as auxiliary coordinates that let the model represent multiple plausible next states from similar current states, avoiding collapse to a mean prediction in the finite-sample size regime. At inference, fresh labels are sampled at each time step and the learned map is rolled forward autoregressively, generating diverse trajectories with a single network evaluation per time step.

</details>


### [478] [Auditing Training Data in Generative Music Models via Black-Box Membership Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29202)
*Yi Chen Liu,Jiawei Yu,Kexin Cao,Syed Irfan Ali Meerza,Trishika Movva,Jian Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The study presents a method for black-box membership inference in generative music models, achieving high accuracy in identifying whether a music sample was used for training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address concerns regarding data provenance, consent, and training transparency in text-to-music generation models.

Method: The method involves querying a target music generation model with a caption, measuring the relationship between the target audio and model output in a learned feature space, and training an auditor to classify membership using paired examples from shadow models.

Result: Achieved up to 98.6% accuracy in identifying training membership, with low false-positive (1.9%) and false-negative (1.0%) rates.

Conclusion: The study demonstrates that reliable auditing of training data is feasible in black-box scenarios for generative music models.

Abstract: Recent advances in text-to-music generation enable high-fidelity synthesis of structured musical audio, raising growing concerns about data provenance, consent, and training transparency. These models are typically trained on large-scale corpora with little disclosure, leaving no practical mechanism to verify whether a particular audio sample was included in training. In this paper, we investigate black-box membership inference for generative music models, aiming to determine whether a candidate music sample was used during training, given only query access to the deployed system. Our key insight is that training membership induces systematically stronger semantic and structural alignment between a candidate sample and the model's generation conditioned on its caption. We query the target model with the associated caption and measure the relationship between the candidate audio and the generated output in a learned feature space. To capture features that separate members from non-members, we construct paired examples consisting of each track and its caption-conditioned generation from shadow models, and train a music auditor to classify membership. The auditor captures alignment patterns characteristic of training membership and generalizes to unseen target models in a fully black-box setting without access to model parameters or training metadata. Across multiple state-of-the-art music generators, our method achieves up to 98.6% accuracy, with false-positive and false-negative rates as low as 1.9% and 1.0%, demonstrating that reliable training-data auditing is feasible in realistic deployment scenarios.

</details>


### [479] [Inferring the Size of Large Language Models From Popular Text Memorization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29223)
*Ivica Nikolic*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper presents a method for inferring lower bounds on the size of large language models (LLMs) using their text generation outputs without needing direct access to their parameters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to provide insights into LLM capabilities and costs, which are typically obscured by developers withholding model sizes.

Method: The method involves analyzing how well models predict the next word in fragments of widely circulated texts, using a memorization signal aggregated into accuracy profile vectors. It employs statistical tests and PCA for inference.

Result: The proposed methods yield accurate lower bounds on model size, validated across a range of open-weight models, and can reveal internal structures of closed-weight models.

Conclusion: The framework highlights differences in scaling strategies among LLM developers, indicating that internal design choices can be inferred without direct access to model parameters.

Abstract: The parameter counts of the most widely used large language models (LLMs) are often withheld by their developers, leaving model size -- a primary reference point for interpreting capabilities and costs -- largely undisclosed. We propose a black-box method to infer conservative lower bounds on LLM size from generated text outputs alone, requiring nothing beyond the ability to submit text fragments and observe next-token predictions. Our approach is grounded in a key observation: popular, widely-circulated texts -- such as classical literature, religious texts, and foundational documents -- are present in virtually every large-scale pretraining corpus, and how accurately a model predicts the next word across text fragments of varying length is a reliable signal of how much it has memorized them, which in turn is fundamentally limited by its total parameter count. We aggregate this memorization signal across a diverse corpus of texts and fragment lengths into a single accuracy profile vector per model, and build two complementary inference methods on top of it: a pairwise statistical test that determines which of two models is larger, and a scaling-law estimator that extracts a one-dimensional latent index from these vectors via Principal Component Analysis (PCA) to map the aggregated signal to a parameter count. Validated on a broad set of open-weight models, both methods produce accurate and reliable lower bounds. When applied to popular closed-weight models, our framework recovers internal product hierarchies and reveals a clear divergence in industry scaling strategies: while some developers yield significantly higher bounds indicative of large generational parameter growth, others operate under strict parameter ceilings, demonstrating that hidden design choices can be systematically probed even under strict API limitations.

</details>


### [480] [Traditional machine learning vs. deep learning from dynamic graph representations of proteins' 3D folds in the task of protein structure classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29228)
*Aydin Wells,Francis A. Gatsi,Aaron Striegel,Tijana Milenković*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper evaluates deep learning versus traditional machine learning in protein structure classification using dynamic protein structure networks, finding little accuracy difference but significant speed differences favoring traditional ML.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To assess if automatic deep learning from dynamic PSNs can improve protein structure classification accuracy compared to traditional machine learning techniques.

Method: The study evaluated 72 datasets of dynamic PSNs, comparing the accuracies of deep learning and traditional machine learning models.

Result: Results show that traditional ML and DL perform similarly in terms of classification accuracy across most datasets, but deep learning is significantly slower, being over 10 times more time-consuming on average.

Conclusion: This is the first comparative analysis of traditional ML and DL in the context of dynamic PSN-based protein structure classification, highlighting a trade-off between accuracy and computational efficiency.

Abstract: Protein structure classification (PSC) uses supervised learning to predict a protein's CATH/SCOP(e) class from the protein's sequence or 3D structural feature(s). We already modeled 3D structures as (static) protein structure networks (PSNs), demonstrating the competitiveness of PSN-based features to sequence or direct (i.e. non-network) 3D structural features in the PSC task. More recently, we demonstrated the power of features extracted from dynamic PSNs over features extracted from static PSNs (and thus by transitivity over sequence and direct 3D structural features) in the same task. That dynamic PSN approach used traditional machine learning (ML), combining manual (pre-engineered) features with an off-the-shelf classifier. Here, we evaluate whether automatic deep learning (DL) from the dynamic PSNs yields improvements. Our evaluation on 72 datasets spanning ~44,000 CATH- or SCOPe-labeled dynamic PSNs reveals that in terms of PSC accuracy, traditional ML and DL are (close to) tied for a large majority of the datasets, while DL is on average 10+ times slower. We are the first to evaluate traditional ML vs. DL in the dynamic PSN-based PSC task.

</details>


### [481] [BlockBatch: Multi-Scale Consensus Decoding for Efficient Diffusion Language Model Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29233)
*Xiaoyou Wu,Cheng-Jhih Shih,Binfei Ji,Yong Liu,Yingyan,Lin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces BlockBatch, an online inference framework that utilizes multiple block sizes in diffusion language models, improving inference speed while preserving accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the trade-off between block sizes in diffusion language model inference, where smaller blocks preserve local context but require more steps, while larger blocks reduce steps but may introduce errors.

Method: BlockBatch executes multiple block-size branches in a batched forward pass, using confidence-gated token merging and synchronization techniques to optimize inference.

Result: BlockBatch achieves an average reduction of 26.6% in denoising NFEs and a 1.33x speedup in end-to-end inference over the Fast-dLLM method, while maintaining accuracy.

Conclusion: Diversity in block sizes is a valuable yet underexplored factor for enhancing the efficiency of branch-parallel inference in diffusion language models.

Abstract: Diffusion language models (dLLMs) generate text by iteratively denoising multiple token positions in parallel, offering an attractive alternative to strictly autoregressive decoding. In practice, however, block-wise dLLM inference exposes a difficult granularity trade-off: small blocks preserve local conditioning but require many denoising steps, whereas large blocks expose more parallelism but can make premature commitments and accumulate cache error. Existing acceleration methods typically choose a single block size per request, leaving the complementarity among block sizes unused. We show that block size itself is a useful branching dimension. Different block sizes induce related but non-identical KV-cache trajectories: branches often share an initial prefix, bifurcate at semantically decisive positions, and later agree on syntactically lightweight tokens. Motivated by this structure, we propose BlockBatch, a training-free online inference framework that executes multiple block-size branches for the same request inside a batched forward pass. BlockBatch coordinates these branches through confidence-gated token merging, leader-based synchronization, and periodic full-sequence refreshes that re-anchor local block updates to a globally consistent KV state. Across 3 representative dLLMs and 4 datasets, BlockBatch reduces denoising NFEs by 26.6\% on average and achieves a 1.33$\times$ average end-to-end speedup over Fast-dLLM while preserving accuracy. These results identify block-size diversity as a practical and previously underexplored axis for branch-parallel dLLM inference.

</details>


### [482] [SigmaMedStat: Temporal Signal Modeling for ICU False Alarm Reduction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29236)
*Arunkumar Ramachandran*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SigmaMedStat uses machine learning to evaluate physiological alarms in ICUs to minimize alarm fatigue and improve patient safety.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address alarm fatigue in ICUs, where the majority of alarms are irrelevant, leading to desensitization and potentially missing critical emergencies.

Method: A machine learning framework employing temporal modeling by splitting recordings into chunks, using Continuous Wavelet Transform (CWT) for feature extraction, and a two-layer Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) network for classification evaluation.

Result: Achieved a mean AUC of 0.822 +/- 0.016 through five-fold stratified cross-validation, outperforming a static model which achieved 0.641 AUC.

Conclusion: Temporal chunking and multi-channel signal fusion significantly enhance classification performance, with identified weaknesses in misclassification of specific alarm types.

Abstract: Alarm fatigue in intensive care units (ICUs) is a well documented patient safety crisis. Clinical monitors generate 350 or more alarms per patient per day, out of which 72-99% are clinically irrelevant. Staff desensitization to non-actionable alarms increases the risk of missed true emergencies. This paper presents SigmaMedStat, a machine learning system that evaluates the trustworthiness of physiological alarm signals before clinical action is taken. Four approaches were evaluated on the PhysioNet/Computing in Cardiology Challenge 2015 dataset of 498 four-channel ICU alarm recordings. Primary contribution is a temporal modeling framework that splits each 60 second recording into six consecutive 10-second chunks, and this in turn generates Continuous Wavelet Transform (CWT) scalograms per chunk, encodes each chunk with a shared EfficientNet-B0 encoder, and passes the resulting feature sequence to a two-layer Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) network. Five-fold stratified cross-validation yields a mean AUC of 0.822 +/- 0.016 (95% CI: [0.790,0.853]), compared to 0.641 for a static EfficientNet baseline trained on the full 60-second window. Ablation studies confirm that temporal chunking and multi-channel signal fusion both contribute independently to classification performance. Per-alarm type analysis reveals that Ventricular Flutter is the most accurately classified alarm type (AUC 0.820) while Asystole remains the hardest (AUC 0.722). Error analysis identifies 65 false negatives and 85 high-confidence misclassifications as the primary failure modes. All code and results are publicly available at https://github.com/Arun-K-Ram/sigmamedstat.

</details>


### [483] [KLAS: Using Similarity to Stitch Neural Networks for Improved Accuracy-Efficiency Tradeoffs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29259)
*Debopam Sanyal,Anantharaman Iyer,Alind Khare,Trisha Jain,Akshay Jajoo,Myungjin Lee,Clayton Kerce,Alexey Tumanov*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: KLAS is a novel framework for selecting stitches between pretrained models to optimize accuracy-efficiency tradeoffs, outperforming existing methods without increasing finetuning costs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a need for flexible model selection that optimizes performance on various deployment targets while managing computational budgets.

Method: KLAS automates stitch selection by utilizing KL divergence between intermediate representations of pretrained models, systematically identifying the best stitch configurations from numerous possibilities.

Result: KLAS enhances accuracy-efficiency tradeoffs of stitched networks, achieving up to 1.21% higher top-1 accuracy on ImageNet-1K or a 1.33x reduction in FLOPs while retaining accuracy compared to baselines.

Conclusion: The KLAS framework generalizes and improves stitch selection across model families, providing superior deployment options within the accuracy-efficiency spectrum.

Abstract: Given the wide range of deployment targets, flexible model selection is essential for optimizing performance within a given compute budget. Recent work demonstrates that stitching pretrained models within a model family enables cost-effective interpolation of the accuracy-efficiency tradeoff space. Stitching transforms intermediate activations from one pretrained model into another, producing a new interpolated stitched network. Such networks provide a pool of deployment options along the accuracy-efficiency spectrum. However, existing stitching approaches often yield suboptimal tradeoffs and lack generalizability, as they primarily rely on heuristics to select stitch configurations. We argue that constructing improved accuracy-efficiency tradeoffs requires explicitly capturing and leveraging the similarity between pretrained models being stitched. To this end, we introduce KLAS, a novel stitch selection framework that automates and generalizes stitch selection across model families by leveraging KL divergence between intermediate representations. KLAS identifies the most promising binary stitches from the $O(k^2n^2)$ possibilities for $k$ pretrained models of depth $n$. Through comprehensive experiments, we demonstrate that KLAS improves the accuracy-efficiency curve of stitched models at the same finetuning cost as baselines. KLAS achieves up to $1.21\%$ higher ImageNet-1K top-1 accuracy at the same computational cost, or maintains accuracy with a $1.33\times$ reduction in FLOPs.

</details>


### [484] [Robust Frequency-Calibrated Virtual EEG Channel Generation from Four Frontal Electrodes for Wearable EEG Augmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29263)
*Minghao Xiao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FAVC-Net generates 13 virtual EEG channels from 4 frontal electrodes, improving spectral fidelity and robustness compared to traditional methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the limitations of low-channel EEG systems by generating additional virtual channels for better monitoring and analysis of brain activity.

Method: FAVC-Net employs a combination of techniques including multi-scale source encoding, GATv2 attention refinement, and spectral density calibration to enhance EEG channel generation.

Result: FAVC-Net outperformed existing models on the PRED+CT dataset, reducing log-spectral distance and PSD KL divergence by significant margins while maintaining spectral integrity under perturbations.

Conclusion: The study highlights the potential of virtual EEG channels as augmented data for analysis, with an emphasis on their interpretation as calibrated representations rather than direct recordings.
